questions.stem: A 22-year-old woman presents with a 3-day history of itchy raised red wheals on her trunk and limbs. Each lesion lasts less than 24 hours and resolves without scarring. She had a mild upper respiratory tract infection a week ago. No angioedema, no systemic symptoms. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Transient wheals lasting <24h with a preceding infection are classic for acute spontaneous urticaria. Urticarial vasculitis lesions last >24h and often leave bruising; erythema multiforme has target lesions; angioedema involves deeper swelling; contact dermatitis is eczematous and not fleeting.

questions.stem: A 34-year-old man develops generalised urticaria, lip swelling, and wheezing within 30 minutes after eating peanuts. He has stridor and hypotension. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The combination of acute urticaria with respiratory distress and hypotension defines anaphylaxis. Acute urticaria alone lacks systemic compromise; hereditary angioedema typically lacks urticaria and is bradykinin-mediated; mastocytosis presents with chronic lesions, not acute trigger.

questions.stem: A 16-year-old boy develops itchy hives on his arms and legs immediately after swimming in a cold sea. The lesions resolve within an hour of warming up. He has no other symptoms. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The trigger is specifically cold exposure, typical of cold urticaria. Cholinergic urticaria is induced by heat/exercise; aquagenic by water contact regardless of temperature; solar urticaria by sunlight; pressure urticaria by sustained pressure.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old woman on amoxicillin for a chest infection develops generalised urticaria on day 5 of treatment. Lesions are itchy and transient. No mucosal involvement or blistering. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
options: Acute urticaria due to drug allergy
questions.explanation: Urticaria is a classic IgE-mediated reaction to penicillin. Maculopapular rash is not wheals; erythema multiforme has target lesions; Stevens-Johnson involves mucosal necrosis and blistering; urticarial vasculitis lesions last >24h and often leave purpura.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old woman complains of raised red lines on her skin wherever she scratches or rubs. The lines are itchy and appear within minutes, fading in about 30 minutes. She is otherwise well. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The wheals are induced by physical stroking, characteristic of symptomatic dermographism. Cholinergic urticaria is triggered by heat/exercise; spontaneous urticaria occurs without physical trigger; contact dermatitis is eczematous and not fleeting; vasculitis lesions persist >24h.

questions.stem: A 22-year-old woman presents with generalised itchy wheals that appeared 3 hours after a mild coryzal illness. She is otherwise well with no history of allergy. What is the most appropriate investigation to confirm the diagnosis of acute urticaria?
options: Skin prick test to common inhalant allergens
questions.explanation: Acute urticaria is a clinical diagnosis; routine investigations are not indicated unless there is suspicion of a specific trigger such as drug or food allergy. Viral infections are a common trigger and do not require testing.

questions.stem: A 20-year-old woman develops itchy wheals on her arms within minutes of washing her hands in cold water. The wheals last about 30 minutes and resolve without scarring. Which investigation can confirm the diagnosis?
options: Skin prick test to common allergens
questions.explanation: Cold urticaria is diagnosed by the ice cube test; a wheal appears after applying an ice cube to the forearm for 5 minutes. The other tests are for other physical urticarias or allergic triggers.

questions.stem: A 50-year-old man presents to the emergency department with acute urticaria, wheeze, and hypotension 15 minutes after a wasp sting. His serum tryptase measured 1 hour after onset is 25 mcg/L (normal <11.4). What is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Elevated serum tryptase indicates mast cell degranulation, supporting the diagnosis of anaphylaxis in the appropriate clinical context. Tryptase is not elevated in panic attacks, vasovagal syncope, or hereditary angioedema; systemic mastocytosis would show persistently elevated baseline tryptase.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old man develops urticaria and perioral swelling within 30 minutes of eating strawberries on three separate occasions. He is otherwise well. What is the most appropriate diagnostic test to confirm the cause?
questions.explanation: Specific IgE testing is the first-line investigation for suspected IgE-mediated food allergy; it is safe and does not carry the risk of anaphylaxis from oral challenge or the lower sensitivity of skin prick tests in some settings.

questions.stem: A 40-year-old woman presents with a 2-week history of daily urticaria. She has a history of hypothyroidism. Which investigation is most likely to identify an associated underlying condition?
questions.explanation: Chronic urticaria (lasting >6 weeks) is associated with autoimmune thyroid disease; checking thyroid autoantibodies is recommended. Anti-dsDNA is for SLE, rheumatoid factor for RA, complement for lupus or angioedema, and tryptase for mastocytosis. Although this is acute urticaria, the history of hypothyroidism raises suspicion of an autoimmune link.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old woman presents with a 2-hour history of itchy, raised red wheals on her trunk and limbs. There is no swelling of the lips, tongue, or throat, and no respiratory difficulty. She is otherwise well. What is the most appropriate first-line treatment?
questions.explanation: Acute urticaria without systemic features is managed with a non-sedating antihistamine such as cetirizine. Chlorphenamine is sedating and not first-line. Adrenaline is used only in anaphylaxis. Topical steroids are ineffective for systemic urticaria. Oral steroids are reserved for severe or refractory cases.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old man presents with generalised urticaria and swelling of his lips. His breathing is normal, and his blood pressure is 130/80 mmHg. There is no stridor or wheeze. What is the most appropriate first-line drug treatment?
questions.explanation: In acute urticaria with angioedema but without anaphylaxis, the first-line treatment is a non-sedating antihistamine (loratadine). Adrenaline is only indicated if there is airway compromise or hypotension. Intravenous chlorphenamine is rarely used and is sedating. Oral prednisolone is second-line for severe or persistent angioedema.

questions.stem: A 22-year-old man is stung by a bee and within minutes develops generalised urticaria, stridor, and a blood pressure of 70/40 mmHg. What is the most appropriate first-line drug?
questions.explanation: Anaphylaxis requires immediate intramuscular adrenaline 500 mcg (0.5 mg) into the thigh. Intravenous adrenaline is for experienced teams only and carries risk. Antihistamines and steroids are adjunctive, not first-line for anaphylaxis.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old breastfeeding woman presents with acute urticaria over her arms and legs with mild pruritus. There is no angioedema or respiratory symptoms. What is the most appropriate antihistamine to prescribe?
questions.explanation: In breastfeeding, non-sedating antihistamines such as loratadine or cetirizine are preferred due to minimal transfer into breast milk and reduced sedation. Chlorphenamine is sedating and may affect the infant. Diphenhydramine, cyproheptadine, and promethazine are also sedating and less desirable.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old woman with acute urticaria and marked lip swelling took cetirizine 10 mg two hours ago with no improvement. Her breathing is still normal, and her blood pressure is 125/75 mmHg. What is the most appropriate next step in drug treatment?
questions.explanation: For severe angioedema not responding to a non-sedating antihistamine, a short course of oral prednisolone (e.g., 40 mg daily for 3 days) is recommended. Adrenaline is only indicated if there is airway compromise or hypotension. Repeating the same antihistamine or adding a sedating one is unlikely to help. Intravenous hydrocortisone is not first-line; oral prednisolone is preferred.

questions.stem: A 24-year-old woman presents to ED with a 2-hour history of itchy, erythematous wheals over her trunk and arms after eating peanuts. She has no lip or tongue swelling, no stridor, and oxygen saturation 99% on air. Blood pressure 118/74 mmHg, heart rate 90 bpm. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Refer to immunology for allergy testing
questions.explanation: This is a mild acute urticaria without systemic features. NICE guidelines recommend a non-sedating oral antihistamine as first-line. Cetirizine is appropriate. Adrenaline is reserved for anaphylaxis; steroids are not first-line and allergy testing is not immediate.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old man presents with widespread urticarial wheals for 3 hours, associated with periorbital angioedema but no dyspnoea, stridor, or hypotension. He has had similar episodes before. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Apply a cold compress and observe for 4 hours
Start oral prednisolone 40 mg once daily
questions.explanation: In acute urticaria with angioedema but no anaphylaxis, the immediate treatment is a sedating antihistamine (e.g., chlorphenamine IV/IM/PO) to control symptoms. Adrenaline is not indicated without airway or circulatory compromise. Steroids are not immediate first-line.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old woman collapses in the ED with acute onset of urticaria, stridor, and hypotension (BP 70/40 mmHg) after receiving intravenous contrast. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Call the anaesthetic team for intubation
questions.explanation: This is anaphylaxis with airway and circulatory compromise. The priority is IM adrenaline (0.5 mg 1:1000) as per the UK Resuscitation Council guidelines. IV fluids and steroids are important but secondary; intubation may be needed but adrenaline is the immediate life-saving intervention.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old man with known ACE inhibitor therapy for hypertension develops acute urticaria and marked angioedema affecting his lips and tongue, without stridor or hypotension. He is anxious but breathing comfortably. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Discharge with oral antihistamines and advise to avoid ACE inhibitors
Stop the ACE inhibitor and prescribe an ARB
questions.explanation: In ACE inhibitor-induced angioedema without anaphylaxis, the immediate treatment is a sedating antihistamine (e.g., chlorphenamine). Adrenaline is reserved for airway compromise; stopping the ACE inhibitor is important but not the immediate next action. Steroids are not first-line.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old woman at 12 weeks gestation presents with acute urticaria after taking amoxicillin for a chest infection. She has itchy wheals on her arms and legs, no mucosal involvement, and observations are normal. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Advise to stop amoxicillin and monitor
questions.explanation: In pregnancy, chlorphenamine is the preferred antihistamine due to a long safety record. Non-sedating antihistamines (cetirizine) are second-line. Adrenaline is not indicated; stopping the offending drug is important but the immediate symptomatic treatment is an antihistamine.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old man presents to the emergency department 20 minutes after a bee sting. He has widespread urticarial weals and swelling of his lips. He is coughing and feels 'tight' in his chest. Observations: HR 110, BP 115/75, RR 24, SpO2 97% on air. Which single clinical finding is most indicative that he is developing anaphylaxis?
questions.explanation: Stridor indicates upper airway obstruction, a hallmark of anaphylaxis. While urticaria and lip swelling are common in allergic reactions, stridor signifies life-threatening airway compromise and confirms anaphylaxis. Cough and tachycardia are non-specific.

questions.stem: A 34-year-old woman presents with recurrent, painful, red weals that last up to 36 hours and leave areas of dark discolouration. She also complains of joint aches and low-grade fever. Which single finding on examination is most suggestive of urticarial vasculitis rather than simple urticaria?
options: Weals are predominantly on the trunk
questions.explanation: Hyperpigmentation is characteristic of urticarial vasculitis due to leakage of red blood cells and subsequent hemosiderin deposition. Simple urticaria weals typically resolve without residual changes.

questions.stem: A 32-year-old woman with a history of nut allergy develops acute urticaria and mild periorbital oedema after eating a curry. Observations show HR 115, BP 88/58 mmHg, RR 20, SpO2 98% on air, no stridor or wheeze. Which single observation most strongly indicates that this is anaphylaxis requiring immediate escalation?
questions.explanation: The UK resus criteria define anaphylaxis as hypotension (SBP <90 mmHg or >30% drop from baseline) even without respiratory compromise. Tachycardia is supportive but not diagnostic alone.

questions.stem: A 40-year-old woman presents with sudden onset of painless, non-itchy swelling of her upper lip and tongue, without any urticaria. She has had similar episodes in the past, sometimes triggered by mild dental trauma. Which single historical feature most strongly suggests hereditary angioedema (HAE) rather than allergic angioedema?
options: Response to antihistamines in previous episodes
Onset within minutes of eating a specific food
Family history of similar swelling episodes
questions.explanation: HAE is autosomal dominant, so a positive family history is a key discriminator. HAE rarely responds to antihistamines, and allergic angioedema typically occurs within minutes of allergen exposure with urticaria and sometimes wheeze.

questions.stem: A 5-year-old boy presents with acute urticaria of 12 hours duration. He has a runny nose and mild cough. His mother says he has not eaten any new foods and has no known allergies. Which single clinical feature most strongly suggests that this urticaria is due to a viral infection rather than an allergic trigger?
options: Response to a single dose of cetirizine
Distribution of weals on the trunk
questions.explanation: Fever is common with viral infections and helps differentiate viral-induced urticaria from allergic causes. Allergic urticaria usually has a clear trigger and often presents without fever.
